description:
You are acting as an integration-developer who is preparing a tiny analysis tool-chain that will later be wired into a CI job.  
    A set of mock API response files already exists on the system (see absolute paths below).  
    Your goal is to:  
      1. Parse every JSON file that matches the glob pattern  
         /home/user/api_responses/response_*.json  
      2. Extract three scalar fields from each file:  
            id          (integer)  
            status      (string)  
            latency_ms  (integer)  
      3. Build a single, comma-separated summary file located at  
            /home/user/api_integration_test/summary.csv  
         The file **must exactly** obey the following rules so that automated grading
         can validate it byte-for-byte:  
            • The first row is a header line literally equal to the string  
              id,status,latency_ms  
            • Each subsequent row contains the values for one response file, separated by
              a single comma and **no spaces**.  
            • The rows must be ordered by id in ascending numeric order.  
            • Every row (including the last one) must end with a single UNIX newline (LF).  

         Example (illustration only – generated content must match the real files on disk):  
            id,status,latency_ms␊  
            101,OK,234␊  
            …  

      4. Produce a helper list of the “fast” calls (latency_ms < 500) and write it to  
            /home/user/api_integration_test/fast_responses.txt  
         Format requirements: one id per line, ascending numeric order, *no* header, each
         terminated with a single LF.  

      5. Create a lightweight processing log,  
            /home/user/api_integration_test/process.log  
         Every time your script touches a JSON file it must append a line of the form  
            Processed <file-name>  
         where <file-name> is the base-name only (e.g. “response_1.json”).  
         Order of the lines is not important, timestamps are NOT required, but each line
         has to end with LF and all three file names must be present exactly once.

    The directory /home/user/api_integration_test/ does not exist yet, so create it as part
    of your workflow.  No root privileges are available, so keep everything within the
    user’s home directory.

    Summary of deliverables the grader will check:

      A) /home/user/api_integration_test/summary.csv  
      B) /home/user/api_integration_test/fast_responses.txt  
      C) /home/user/api_integration_test/process.log  

    Make sure the text files’ content and formatting are **precisely** as specified,
    including newlines, header presence (or absence) and ordering, otherwise the automated
    test will fail.
truth:
Initial files already present before the agent starts:

      1. /home/user/api_responses/response_1.json
         {
           "id": 101,
           "status": "OK",
           "latency_ms": 234
         }

      2. /home/user/api_responses/response_2.json
         {
           "id": 102,
           "status": "FAIL",
           "latency_ms": 678
         }

      3. /home/user/api_responses/response_3.json
         {
           "id": 103,
           "status": "OK",
           "latency_ms": 345
         }

    Expected artefacts after successful task completion:

      • /home/user/api_integration_test/summary.csv
        id,status,latency_ms\n
        101,OK,234\n
        102,FAIL,678\n
        103,OK,345\n

      • /home/user/api_integration_test/fast_responses.txt
        101\n
        103\n

      • /home/user/api_integration_test/process.log
        (order of lines may vary, but each line must end with '\n' exactly once)
        Processed response_1.json\n
        Processed response_2.json\n
        Processed response_3.json\n
